key_departures.impact: Aiyuk missed the entire 2025 season with ACL/MCL injuries, stopped communicating with the team, and had his future guarantees voided. GM Lynch confirmed he will not return. Team incurs roughly $29.6M in dead cap (split post-June 1 into ~$13.3M in 2026 and ~$21.2M in 2027). Was the nominal WR1 pre-injury; creates a significant hole even accounting for his lost 2025 season.

Led the 49ers with 9 receiving TDs in 2025 (55 catches, 643 yards) and was their most reliable WR in a season ravaged by injury. Priced himself out of SF's comfort range; Evans signing effectively replaced him. Jennings remains unsigned as of March 16. Departure removes the team's most clutch possession receiver.

Huff announced retirement on March 12, 2026, one day before a $1M roster bonus was due and before the team had to decide on a $14.635M option bonus. Finished 2025 with 4 sacks and 15 QB hits in 15 games but was inconsistent (0 sacks in final 10 regular-season and playoff games). Retirement saves the 49ers $5.392M in 2026 cap space. Leaves a significant void as the pass rusher opposite Nick Bosa.
Void year triggered; departed as UFA. Rotational pass rusher whose loss compounds the EDGE need after Huff's retirement.

Signed 2yr/$8M with Titans (reuniting with HC Robert Saleh). Combined with former starter Kalia Davis for just 0.5 sacks on 516 pass-rush snaps in 2025 — historically bad interior DL production. Departure frees up a need the team was already urgently addressing.

49ers declined to tender Davis as an RFA; he signed a 1yr/$3M deal with Cleveland. Was a primary DT starter in 2025 but combined with Elliott for negligible interior pass rush. Departure paired with Elliott's exit forced the team to trade for Odighizuwa.
Signed 2yr/$12M with NFC West rival Cardinals. Was a rotational WR and locker-room leader; departure adds to the receiver room reshaping but was not a starter-caliber loss.

Departed in the 2025 offseason (prior FA class) to Green Bay. Listed here for context as a comp pick driver (projected 4th-round comp pick). His loss left a void at left guard that the 49ers have not yet addressed with a starter-caliber signing.
Departed as UFA. Was the primary LG in 2025. Leaves a hole at interior OL that the team has not addressed beyond depth signings.
Depth center departed. Compounds interior OL depth concerns but wasn't a starter.
49ers declined to tender Beal as an RFA; signed 1yr deal with Dolphins. Rotational EDGE loss adds to pass-rush depth concerns.
Depth safety departed. Minimal impact on starting lineup but reduces secondary depth.
Signed 1yr/$4.5M with Packers. Depth WR loss; minor impact on a room being rebuilt anyway.

signings.analysis: Marquee signing of the offseason. Evans posted 11 consecutive 1,000-yard seasons before injury limited him to 8 games in 2025. Three-year deal at ~$20M AAV is solid starter money, and the 3-year commitment signals genuine investment. However, Evans is 32 coming off an injury-plagued season, one of the top WR needs (a second WR to complement Pearsall/Evans) remains unresolved, and Jennings and Aiyuk are both gone. WR need drops significantly — Evans addresses WR1 — but the team still needs a WR2 opposite him. Partially addressed rather than fully, given the continued depth hole and Evans' age. Team will likely still target WR in the draft.
signings.notes: Contract guaranteed amount not yet publicly reported. AAV positions Evans at solid starter tier for the position.

signings.analysis: The most impactful defensive move of the offseason. SF traded their 3rd-round pick (No. 92) for Odighizuwa, who is under contract through 2028 with base salaries of $16.25M (fully guaranteed in 2026), $20M, and $20M. That 3-year, fully-on-the-books deal is a real commitment. Odighizuwa ranked 1st among DTs in QB hits (23) in 2025 despite just 3.5 sacks — elite interior pass rusher profile. He replaces the historically bad interior production of Elliott/Davis (0.5 sacks combined). The 49ers surrendered a Day 2 pick but gained a proven starter at their biggest defensive need under contract for 3 years. IDL need fully addressed at the starter level. Note: $56.25M total value is approximated from the 3-year remaining contract structure ($16.25M + $20M + $20M).

Greenlaw returns to San Francisco after one tumultuous year in Denver. One-year, $7.5M deal is a reasonable prove-it contract for a player who has played just 10 games since tearing his Achilles in Super Bowl LVIII. If healthy, he reunites with Fred Warner to form one of the best LB duos in the NFC; if he misses time again, the 49ers have Dee Winters, Tatum Bethune, and Nick Martin as fallback options. One-year structure limits this to partially-addressed — the team still needs to find a long-term LB2 answer beyond 2026. Guaranteed money not yet disclosed; estimated modest given 1-year bridge structure.

Re-signed before free agency opened. Piñeiro went 28-of-29 on field goals in 2025 (96.6% — league-leading) with 6-of-7 from 50+. The 4yr/$17M deal with $10M guaranteed locks up the team's most reliable specialist since Robbie Gould. Kicker is no longer a need for the foreseeable future. Signing signals strong organizational confidence.

One-year prove-it deal after the Packers released Hobbs following an injury-plagued 2025 (only 221 snaps). Was signed to a 4yr/$48M deal by Green Bay just a year ago, so the talent ceiling is real but availability is a major concern. Projects as depth/competition opposite Deommodore Lenoir, potentially pushing Renardo Green (who fell out of favor in the playoffs). At $4.5M on a 1-year deal, this is a low-cost upside play — not a solution to the CB2 need. CB remains a moderate draft need.

Two-year depth signing at swing tackle. Lowe has 25 career starts over 3 seasons with New England, including 4 starts at LT in 2025. Projects as a swing tackle behind Colton McKivitz at RT, with potential to play left if Trent Williams situation is resolved via trade/retirement. At $6M AAV this is well below starter money. Does NOT address the critical Trent Williams contract impasse or the need for a true LT heir apparent. OT need remains elevated pending Williams' 2026 status resolution.

One-year depth deal for a swing interior lineman who can play both guard spots and center. Projects as a swing/backup with a chance to compete for a starting spot at LG, which is currently the 49ers' biggest unaddressed IOL need post-Burford/Banks. Doesn't move the needle on the need — the team still needs a quality starting guard.
signings.notes: Contract terms not publicly disclosed as of March 16, 2026.

signings.analysis: Re-signed as TE2 behind George Kittle (recovering from a torn Achilles suffered in January 2026). Tonges emerged from nowhere in 2025 with 34 catches, 293 yards, and 5 TDs when Kittle was injured. Two-year deal provides continuity and insurance as Kittle's return timeline remains uncertain. This doesn't fully address the TE need because Kittle is a legitimate injury concern for 2026. Tonges is a trustworthy backup — the team may still want another TE via draft.
signings.notes: Contract total value not publicly disclosed as of March 16, 2026.

signings.analysis: Pro Bowl special teamer re-signed. Gifford tied for 6th in special teams tackles in 2025 (20). Re-signing maintains special teams continuity and costs minimal cap. With Greenlaw back, Gifford's defensive role should diminish. No impact on starting depth chart needs.
Veteran long snapper re-signed on 1-year extension. Maintains special teams stability. Not a positional need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Odighizuwa trade (3yr/$56M remaining, fully guaranteed $16.25M in 2026) fully addresses the catastrophically weak interior that totaled 0.5 sacks in 2025. He ranked 1st in DT QB hits last year. The 49ers surrendered a 3rd-round pick and inherited 3 years of contract, signaling real commitment. IDL drops from critical need to luxury pick — a developmental backup would still be welcome but this is no longer a draft priority.

Evans signing (3yr/$60.4M, ~$20M AAV) addresses the WR1 vacancy left by Aiyuk's departure and Jennings' departure. However, with both Aiyuk and Jennings gone plus Bourne leaving, the team has Evans + Ricky Pearsall (injury-limited in 2025) + demarcus Robinson/Jacob Cowing/Jordan Watkins as depth. Evans is 32 coming off an injury year. The WR room is still thin and lacks a proven WR2. Drops from critical tier 1 to elevated tier 2 — still a strong draft priority in rounds 1-3.

Net escalation: Huff retired (removing the primary EDGE2 opposite Bosa), Yetur Gross-Matos departed as UFA, Robert Beal Jr. departed to Miami. The 49ers added NOTHING at EDGE in free agency. Nick Bosa (restructured contract) remains elite but the team finished LAST in the NFL in sacks (20) in 2025 and 31st in pressures. With Huff gone, there is no established pass rusher opposite Bosa. EDGE is now the single most critical unaddressed need entering the draft. Escalated to tier 1 — premium draft target in round 1 or 2.

Aaron Banks departed last offseason to Green Bay (driving a comp pick). Spencer Burford (LG) departed this offseason. Only depth signings added: Brett Toth (1yr, minimal guaranteed, swing interior depth). The starting guard spot(s) remain unresolved. Toth provides competition/depth but is not a starter-quality answer at LG. IOL need stays elevated at tier 2 — team should target a starting guard in rounds 2-4.

Trent Williams' contract situation (38 in July, $38.84M 2026 cap hit, ongoing standoff) remains completely unresolved as of March 16. Williams could be restructured, traded, or retired. Vederian Lowe (2yr/$12M) adds depth/swing tackle capability but is NOT a replacement for Williams at LT if Williams departs or misses time. OT need remains tier 2 and could escalate to tier 1 if Williams is traded or retires before the draft.

Hobbs signing (1yr/$4.5M) is a depth/competition move, not a solution. He played only 221 snaps for Green Bay in 2025 due to injuries and is coming off a career year that ended abruptly. Deommodore Lenoir is the CB1; CB2 between Renardo Green and Hobbs remains a legitimate competition. CB remains a tier 2 draft need — team should target a CB in rounds 2-4.

Greenlaw re-signing (1yr/$7.5M) is a high-upside move but a bridge — his injury history (just 10 games played since Super Bowl LVIII) makes this a gamble. If healthy alongside Fred Warner, LB is a strength. But the 1-year deal means this need resurfaces in 2027. Unchanged at tier 3 — consider a developmental LB in rounds 4-6 as insurance.

Tonges re-signing provides legitimate TE2 depth behind Kittle, who tore his Achilles in January 2026 and faces an uncertain return timeline for the 2026 season opener. Tonges proved capable (34/293/5 TD in 2025). If Kittle misses significant time, the need escalates. For now, TE stays tier 3 — monitor Kittle's recovery; a TE via draft Day 2-3 would be prudent.

Jason Pinnock departed to Giants (depth loss). No meaningful additions at safety. The 49ers' safety room lacks a proven starter opposite whoever they deploy in the post-Hufanga era. Unchanged at tier 3 — addressable in rounds 3-5 in the draft.

key_additions.headline: Future Hall of Famer (11 straight 1,000-yard seasons) departs Tampa after 12 years to become the 49ers' new WR1 opposite Ricky Pearsall

key_additions.contract_summary: 3yr remaining/$56.25M ($16.25M gtd in 2026); via trade (gave up 3rd-round pick No. 92)
key_additions.headline: Trade acquisition addresses the 49ers' catastrophic interior pass rush — Odighizuwa led all DTs with 23 QB hits in 2025 and is under contract through 2028
Fan-favorite LB returns to reunite with Fred Warner after one injury-shortened year in Denver — high-upside reclamation if healthy
Re-signed before free agency: Piñeiro went 28-of-29 on FGs in 2025 (96.6%, NFL-best) and locks in long-term stability at kicker
One-year upside play on a 26-year-old CB with elite run-defense grades who was released by Green Bay after injury-plagued 2025
Swing tackle depth signing provides OL flexibility as Trent Williams' contract situation remains unresolved
Re-signed as TE2 insurance while Kittle recovers from torn Achilles — Tonges posted 34/293/5 TD in Kittle's absence in 2025

key_losses.headline: 49ers officially part ways with Aiyuk after he missed all of 2025 with ACL/MCL injuries and cut off communication with the team — ~$29.6M in dead cap
Team's most reliable WR in 2025 (9 TDs, 55 catches) prices himself out of SF range after Evans signing — still unsigned on the open market
27-year-old edge rusher shockingly retires, leaving a gaping hole opposite Nick Bosa with no FA replacement signed — SF's most critical remaining draft need
Starting DT departs to reunite with HC Robert Saleh in Tennessee — his departure (alongside Davis) prompted the Odighizuwa trade
Fan favorite and locker-room leader signs with NFC West rival Cardinals on a 2yr/$12M deal
Starting LG departs as UFA, leaving San Francisco with an unaddressed hole at left guard heading into the draft

additional_notes.trent_williams_situation: Trent Williams (age 38 in July 2026) carries a $38.84M cap hit in 2026. The 49ers have been unable to agree on a contract restructure. Williams' camp has been granted permission to explore trade options. This situation is UNRESOLVED as of March 16, 2026 and could dramatically escalate OT to a tier-1 need if he is traded or retires.
additional_notes.bosa_restructure: The 49ers restructured Nick Bosa's contract, clearing approximately $17.172M in 2026 cap space. Bosa remains set to earn $23.18M in 2026 with the move pushing money into future years.
additional_notes.draft_capital_note: The 49ers surrendered their 2026 3rd-round pick (No. 92) in the Odighizuwa trade, leaving them with approximately 6 draft picks heading into the 2026 NFL Draft. This limits draft-based fixes to the EDGE and other needs.
additional_notes.sam_okuayinonu_tender: The 49ers tendered RFA DE Sam Okuayinonu at the original-round level (1yr, $3.546M), retaining him as EDGE depth.
